elements.sent-2411-001: Let $G$ be a bipartite graph with adjacency matrix $A(G)$.
elements.sent-2411-002: The characteristic polynomial $\phi(G,x)=\det(xI-A(G))$ and the permanental polynomial $\pi(G,x) = \per(xI-A(G))$ are both graph invariants used to distinguish graphs.
elements.sent-2411-003: For bipartite graphs, we define the modified characteristic polynomial, which is obtained by changing the signs of some of the coefficients of $\phi(G,x)$.
elements.sent-2411-004: For $4k$-intercyclic bipartite graphs, i.e., those for which the removal of any $4k$-cycle results in a $C_{4k}$-free graph, we provide an expression for $\pi(G,x)$ in terms of the modified characteristic polynomial of the graph and its subgraphs.
elements.sent-2411-005: Our approach is purely combinatorial in contrast to the Pfaffian orientation method found in the literature to compute the permanental polynomial.
elements.sent-2411-006: Released under the CC BY 4.0 International License \renewcommand{\thefootnote {\arabic{footnote.}}}
elements.sent-2411-007: We consider simple and undirected graphs.
elements.sent-2411-008: Let $G$ be a graph with the vertex set $V(G)=\{v_1,v_2,\dots,v_n\}$.
elements.sent-2411-009: The adjacency matrix $A(G)=(a_{i,j )$ of a graph $G$ is defined such that $a_{i,j = 1$ if $v_i$ and $v_j$ are adjacent and $0$ otherwise where $i, j \in \{1,2,\dots, n\}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-010: The determinant and the permanent of $A(G)$, are defined as \begin{equation}\det (A(G))=\sum_{\sigma \in S_n}\sign(\sigma)\prod_{i=1}^{n}a_{i,\sigma(i)} \text{ and }\per(A(G))=\sum_{\sigma \in S_n}\prod_{i=1}^{n}a_{i,\sigma(i)},\end{equation} respectively, where $S_n$ is the set of all permutations of the set $\{1,2,\dots, n\}$ and $\sign(\sigma)$ is the signature of the permutation $\sigma$.
elements.sent-2411-011: While the determinant can be computed in polynomial time using the Gaussian elimination method, and the fastest known algorithm runs in $\mathcal{O (n^{2.371552 )$ time \cite{aho1974design,williams2024new , computing the permanent is notoriously difficult, as it is $\#$P-complete \cite{valiant1979complexity .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: The ``Permanent vs. Determinant Problem'' about symbolic matrices in computational complexity theory is as follows: ``Can we express the permanent of a matrix as the determinant of a (possibly polynomially larger) matrix?'' For an upper bound on the size of the larger matrix, see \cite{grenet2011upper , and for a survey on lower bounds, see \cite{agrawal2006determinant .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: The problem ``Given a $(0,1)$-matrix $A$, under what conditions, changing the sign of some the nonzero entries yields a matrix $B$ such that $\per(A)=\det(B)$?'' is famously known as ``Polya Permanent Problem,'' \cite{polya_aufgabe_1913 and it is equivalent to twenty-three other problems listed in \cite{mccuaig_polyas_2004 .}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Immanants are matrix functions that generalize determinant and permanent, and their complexity dichotomy was also recently studied \cite{curticapean2021full .}
elements.sent-2411-015: The characteristic polynomial and the permanental polynomial of graph $G$ on $n$ vertices are univariate polynomials defined as \begin{equation}\phi(G,x)=\det(xI-A(G))\text{ and } \pi(G,x)=\per(xI-A(G)),\end{equation} respectively, where $I$ is the identity matrix of order $n$.
elements.sent-2411-016: The characteristic and the permanental polynomials are graph invariants, and they can be used to distinguish graphs towards Graph Isomorphism Problem \cite{vandam_haemers .}
elements.sent-2411-017: But the permanental polynomial is not studied in great detail as compared to the characteristic polynomial, probably due to its computational difficulty.
elements.sent-2411-018: Some computational evidence suggests that the permanental polynomial is better than the characteristic polynomial while distinguishing graphs \cite{dehmer2017highly,liu2014enumeration .}
elements.sent-2411-019: We are interested in finding ways to compute the permanental polynomial efficiently; one way to do that is by expressing the permanental polynomial in terms of the characteristic polynomial.
elements.sent-2411-020: For an excellent survey on the permanental polynomial, we refer to \cite{2016permanental .. Let \begin{equation}\phi(G,x)=\sum_{i=0}^{n} a_ix^{n-i}\text{ and } \pi(G,x)=\sum_{i=0}^{n} b_ix^{n-i}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-021: The interpretation of these coefficients is given as \begin{equation}\label{eqn:sachs_interp} a_i = \sum_{U_i} (-1)^{p(U_i)} 2^{c(U_i)} \text{ \cite{sachs1964beziehungen} }, \;\; b_i = (-1)^i\sum_{U_i} 2^{c(U_i)} \text{ \cite{merris1981permanental} },\end{equation} where the summation is taken over all the Sachs subgraphs $U_i$ (subgraphs whose components are either cycles or edges) of $G$ on $i$ vertices, $p(U_i)$ denotes the number of components in $U_i$, and $c(U_i)$ denotes the number of components in $U_i$ that are cycles.
elements.sent-2411-022: It is easy to see the following.
elements.sent-2411-023: Hence, for a bipartite graph $G$, we have \begin{equation}\label{eqn:bip_exp} \phi(G,x)=\sum_{i=0,2,4,\dots} a_{i}x^{n-i}\text{ and } \pi(G,x)=\sum_{i=0,2,4, \dots} b_{i}x^{n-i}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-024: Defining $f_i= b_i - (-1)^{i/2 a_i$ for each nonnegative even integer $i$, we introduce a modified characteristic polynomial and also a new graph polynomial for bipartite graphs as \begin{equation}\phi_p(G,x)=\sum_{i=0,2,4,\dots} (-1)^{i/2}a_ix^{n-i}\text{ and } f(G,x)=\sum_{i=0,2,4,\dots} f_ix^{n-i},\end{equation} respectively, such that we have \begin{equation}\label{eqn:pi=phi+f} \pi(G,x) = \phi_p(G,x) + f(G,x).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-025: We denote a cycle of length $k$ by $C_{k}$.
elements.sent-2411-026: For each positive integer $k$, we refer to a $C_{4k}$ by a $4k$-cycle.
elements.sent-2411-027: A graph is called $C_{4k}$-free if it does not contain a $4k$-cycle for all positive integers $k$.
elements.sent-2411-028: A graph is called $4k$-intercyclic if it does not contain two vertex-disjoint $4k$-cycles (these $4k$-cycles could be of different length).
elements.sent-2411-029: Equivalently, after removal of the vertices of any $4k$-cycle from a $4k$-intercyclic graph, the resultant graph is $C_{4k}$-free (see Figure \ref{fig:pap_ex5 for an example).}
elements.sent-2411-031: Then, $G$ is $C_{4k}$-free if and only if its per-spectrum is $\{i\lambda_1,i\lambda_2,\dots, i\lambda_n\}$.\footnote{The spectrum and the per-spectrum of a graph $G$ are the multiset of all roots of its characteristic polynomial and the permanental polynomial, respectively, and $i$ is an imaginary unit.}
elements.sent-2411-032: By inspecting the proof of this theorem, we notice that a bipartite graph $G$ is $C_{4k}$-free if and only if $\pi(G,x) = \phi_p(G,x)$ (see Corollary \ref{cor:4kbip ).}
elements.sent-2411-033: As a result, the permanental polynomial of $C_{4k}$-free bipartite graphs can be computed directly through the modified characteristic polynomial.
elements.sent-2411-034: Yan and Zhang, in 2004, found that the permanental polynomial of a larger class of bipartite graphs can be computed using the characteristic polynomial of some oriented graph.
elements.sent-2411-036: Then there exists an orientation $G^e$ of $G$ such that $\pi(G, x) = \det(xI - A(G^e)),$ where $A(G^e)$ denotes the skew adjacency matrix of $G^e$.
elements.sent-2411-037: Later Zhang and Li, in 2012, proved the converse of this statement.
elements.sent-2411-038: For the definition of an even subdivision of a graph, see \cite{yan2004permanental,zhang2012computing .}
elements.sent-2411-039: Zhang and Li also show that bipartite graphs that do not contain an even subdivision of $K_{2,3}$ are planar and admit Pfaffian orientation.
elements.sent-2411-040: They also give characterization and recognition of such graphs, which leads to a polynomial time algorithm to compute the permanental polynomial of such bipartite graphs.
elements.sent-2411-042: There exists an orientation $G^e$ of a graph $G$ such that $\pi(G, x) = \det(xI - A(G^e))$ if and only if $G$ is a bipartite graph containing no even subdivision of $K_{2,3}$.

elements.sent-2411-051: In this article, we give a formula to compute $\pi(G,x)$ for the class of $4k$-intercyclic bipartite graphs (a superset of the class of $C_{4k}$-free bipartite graphs).
elements.sent-2411-052: This is done by expressing $f(G,x)$ in terms of the modified characteristic polynomial of the subgraphs of $G$.
elements.sent-2411-053: Our approach is combinatorial rather than based on Pfaffian orientation.
elements.sent-2411-054: Note that the class of $4k$-intercyclic bipartite graphs is different from and not a subset of the class of bipartite graphs that do not contain an even subdivision of $K_{2,3}$.

elements.sent-2411-095: Hence, the permanental polynomial of a $4k$-intercyclic bipartite graph can be computed in polynomial time if the length of the largest $4k$ cycle is bounded by $\log n$ or if the number of cycles is bounded by a polynomial in $n$.
elements.sent-2411-096: We now discuss an application of Theorem \ref{thm:boro_gen in constructing cospectral graphs.}
elements.sent-2411-097: Recall that two graphs $G_1$ and $G_2$ are said to be cospectral if they have the same characteristic polynomial, that is, $\phi(G_1,x)=\phi(G_2,x)$.
elements.sent-2411-098: Similarly, we say that they are per-cospectral if they have the same permanental polynomial, that is, $\pi(G_1,x)=\pi(G_2,x)$.
elements.sent-2411-099: Since $\phi_p(G,x)$ can be recovered from $\phi(G,x)$, it follows from Corollary \ref{cor:4kbip that two $C_{4k}$-free bipartite graphs $G_1$ and $G_2$ are cospectral if and only if they are per-cospectral \cite{borowiecki1985spectrum .}}

elements.sent-2411-106: But since $G_1, G_2 \in \mathcal{G _p$, we have $f(G_1,x) = f(G_2,x)$.}
